_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Strings
summary: Store text, read individual characters, and build new strings.
refresher: `std::string` stores text. Indexes begin at zero, `.size()` gives the character count, and `+` joins compatible strings. Include `<string>` before using this type.

blocks.body: Use `std::string` for words and sentences. Include `<string>`, then create a value such as `std::string name = "Ada";`. A single `char` stores only one character, while a string stores many.

String positions start at zero. In `"code"`, index zero is `c` and index three is `e`. `.size()` returns the number of characters, so the final valid index is one less than the size. Check that a string is not empty before asking for its last character.

blocks.title: Strings feel familiar from Python
blocks.body: Python and C++ strings both start indexing at zero. Python writes `len(word)`, while C++ writes `word.size()`. C++ also places the type in the `std` library namespace, which is why its full name is `std::string`.

blocks.caption: The first bracket reads the first character. substr starts at index one and takes three characters.
Check that text exists before reading its final character.
blocks.wrong: char last = text[text.size() - 1]; // unsafe when empty

blocks.body: For a nonempty word, `back()` returns the final character. An empty string has no final character, so the `if` condition skips that access. Remember that a size is a count, not a position.

blocks.title: The size is one past the last index
blocks.body: A four-character string has a size of four but valid indexes from zero through three. `text[text.size()]` goes one position beyond the string.

blocks.why: The string contains three characters, so its size is three.
The call starts at index one and takes two characters, producing od.

blocks.title: Strings checklist
items: Check emptiness before using the last character.
